refashioned:
Introduction and Plan of the Work. Every nation's annual labor is the fund that supplies all the necessities and comforts of life it consumes each year. This supply consists either of what that labor directly produces, or of what is bought with that produce from other nations.

A nation will therefore be better or worse supplied with the necessities and comforts it needs, depending on whether this produce -- or what is purchased with it -- is large or small relative to the number of people who must consume it.
elegant: A nation's annual labor is the source of everything it consumes -- either directly produced or traded for. National prosperity depends on the ratio of this total produce to the population that must live on it.
summary: A nation's yearly labor is the fundamental source of all goods it consumes. Its standard of living depends on the ratio of total output to the number of people consuming it.
distilled: National wealth is determined by the ratio of a country's total annual output to its population.
notes: Opens the entire Wealth of Nations -- frames the central question as output per capita, not total output. 'Necessaries and conveniencies' is Smith's recurring phrase for the full spectrum of goods. Establishes that trade (purchasing from other nations) is part of the national supply. This per-capita framing anticipates modern GDP per capita as a welfare measure.

refashioned: This ratio of output to population must be determined by two factors in every nation. First, by the skill, dexterity, and judgment with which its labor is generally applied. Second, by the proportion of people engaged in useful labor versus those who are not. Regardless of a nation's soil, climate, or territory, the abundance or scarcity of its annual supply must, given its particular situation, depend on these two factors.
elegant: Two factors determine a nation's output-to-population ratio: the productivity of its labor, and the share of the population engaged in useful work. These matter more than natural endowments like soil, climate, or territory.
summary: National prosperity depends on two things: how productively labor is applied, and what share of the population is engaged in productive work. These matter more than natural resources or geography.
distilled: A nation's wealth depends on labor productivity and the proportion of its people doing productive work, not on its natural endowments.
notes: Introduces the two master variables of the entire work: labor productivity and the productive-to-unproductive labor ratio. Explicitly dismisses geography and natural resources as secondary -- a radical claim for the 18th century. 'Skill, dexterity, and judgment' foreshadows Book I's analysis of the division of labor. The productive vs. unproductive labor distinction becomes a major theme in Book II.

refashioned: Of these two factors, the abundance or scarcity of a nation's supply seems to depend more on the first -- labor productivity -- than on the second. Among primitive hunter-gatherer societies, nearly every able-bodied person is engaged in useful labor, working to provide necessities and comforts for themselves, their families, and those too old, young, or sick to hunt and fish. Yet these societies are so desperately poor that, from sheer deprivation, they are frequently driven -- or at least believe themselves driven -- to the necessity of sometimes directly killing, and sometimes abandoning, their infants, elderly, and chronically ill to die of starvation or be consumed by wild animals. Among civilized and prosperous nations, by contrast, a large number of people do no productive work at all, many of whom consume the output of ten times, often a hundred times, more labor than most workers produce. Yet the total output of society is so enormous that everyone is often abundantly supplied. Even a worker of the lowest and poorest class, if frugal and industrious, can enjoy a greater share of life's necessities and comforts than any person in a primitive society could ever obtain.
elegant: Labor productivity matters more than the employment ratio. In primitive societies, nearly everyone works, yet they remain so poor they sometimes abandon their vulnerable members to die. In advanced societies, many people do no productive work at all -- some consuming a hundred times more than workers produce -- yet total output is so vast that even the poorest industrious worker lives better than any person in a primitive society ever could.
summary: Labor productivity is the more important of the two factors. Primitive societies have near-total employment yet extreme poverty, while advanced nations carry many non-workers yet produce so abundantly that even the poorest laborer lives better than anyone in a primitive society.
distilled: Productivity of labor matters far more than the share of people working -- advanced nations with many idle members still vastly outproduce primitive societies where everyone works.
notes: Smith's most striking illustration of the productivity thesis -- near-universal employment in primitive societies produces worse outcomes than partial employment in advanced ones. The comparison anticipates modern development economics. The claim that a poor worker in a civilized nation is better off than anyone in a primitive one is sometimes called 'Smith's optimism' -- a precursor to arguments about absolute vs. relative poverty. The 'hundred times more labour' consumed by the idle rich introduces the inequality theme explored in Book I, Chapters 6-11.

refashioned:
The causes of this improvement in the productive powers of labor, and the natural order in which its output is distributed among the different ranks and classes of society, are the subject of the first book of this inquiry.

Whatever the actual state of skill, dexterity, and judgment with which labor is applied in any nation, the abundance or scarcity of its annual supply must also depend on the ratio of those employed in useful labor to those who are not. The number of useful and productive workers, as will be shown later, is everywhere proportional to the amount of capital employed in putting them to work, and to the particular way that capital is deployed. The second book, therefore, deals with the nature of capital, how it is gradually accumulated, and the different amounts of labor it sets in motion depending on how it is employed.
elegant: Book I examines what improves labor productivity and how output is naturally distributed across social classes. But the second factor -- the ratio of productive to unproductive workers -- also matters. The number of productive workers depends on the quantity of capital available to employ them and how that capital is used. Book II therefore treats the nature, accumulation, and deployment of capital.
summary: Book I covers the causes of labor productivity and how output is distributed across classes. Book II addresses capital -- its nature, accumulation, and how it determines the number of productive workers and the way they are employed.
distilled: Book I explains labor productivity and income distribution; Book II explains how capital accumulation determines the scale and direction of productive employment.
notes: Roadmap entry for Books I and II. Links capital directly to productive employment -- capital is what 'sets labor to work.' This capital-labor relationship is foundational to classical economics. The phrase 'naturally distributed' signals Smith's belief in a natural order of income distribution across wages, profits, and rent (Book I, Chapters 6-11). 'Gradually accumulated' hints at Smith's theory of saving and investment in Book II.

refashioned: Nations that have become reasonably advanced in the skill and judgment applied to their labor have nonetheless followed very different policies in directing that labor, and these policies have not been equally favorable to maximizing output. Some nations' policies have given extraordinary encouragement to rural industry; others have favored the industry of towns. Hardly any nation has treated every kind of industry equally and impartially. Since the fall of the Roman Empire, European policy has favored arts, manufactures, and commerce -- the industry of towns -- over agriculture, the industry of the countryside. The circumstances that introduced and established this policy are explained in the third book.
elegant: Advanced nations have pursued very different economic policies, and not all have been equally good for output. Some have favored rural industry, others urban industry; almost none have treated all industries impartially. Since Rome's fall, Europe has consistently favored urban manufactures and commerce over agriculture. Book III explains how this bias arose.
summary: Nations have adopted different economic policies that favor different industries, and none has been truly impartial. Since the fall of Rome, Europe has systematically favored urban commerce and manufactures over agriculture. Book III examines the origins of this bias.
distilled: European economic policy since Rome has systematically favored urban industry over agriculture, and Book III explains why.
notes: Roadmap entry for Book III. Introduces the urban-rural policy bias as a central distortion in European economic history. 'Equally and impartially' signals Smith's free-market ideal -- that government should not pick winners among industries. The post-Roman framing sets up the historical narrative of Book III (feudalism, town charters, the rise of commerce). Foreshadows the critique of mercantilism in Book IV.

refashioned: Although these different policies were probably first introduced by the private interests and prejudices of particular groups, with no regard for or foresight of their consequences for the general welfare of society, they have given rise to very different theories of political economy. Some of these theories emphasize the importance of urban industry; others champion rural industry. These theories have significantly influenced not only the opinions of scholars, but also the policies of princes and governments. In the fourth book, I have attempted to explain these different theories as fully and clearly as I can, along with the principal effects they have produced in different eras and nations.
elegant: These biased policies, originally driven by the self-interest of particular groups rather than concern for general welfare, spawned rival theories of political economy -- some favoring urban industry, others rural. These theories have shaped both intellectual opinion and government policy. Book IV examines these theories and their real-world effects across different eras and nations.
summary: Biased economic policies, born from special interests rather than public welfare, generated competing theories of political economy that have influenced both scholars and governments. Book IV analyzes these theories and their historical effects.
distilled: Book IV critiques the competing economic theories -- favoring either urban or rural industry -- that arose from special-interest policies and shaped government action.
notes: Roadmap entry for Book IV. 'Private interests and prejudices of particular orders of men' -- Smith's recurring theme that policy is driven by rent-seeking, not public interest. The two rival theories are mercantilism (urban/commercial focus) and physiocracy (agricultural focus). Smith's framing -- theories born from distorted policy, not from truth -- signals his critical stance toward both. 'Princes and sovereign states' indicates Smith is writing partly as a policy advisor.

refashioned: The object of these first four books is to explain what has constituted the revenue of the great body of the people -- the nature of those funds that have supplied their annual consumption in different eras and nations. The fifth and final book deals with the revenue of the sovereign, or the state. In this book I have attempted to show: first, what the necessary expenses of the state are, and which of those should be paid for by the whole society versus by particular groups or individuals; second, the different methods by which society can be made to contribute toward expenses that fall on everyone, along with the main advantages and disadvantages of each method; and third and finally, what reasons and causes have led almost all modern governments to mortgage some part of this revenue, or to take on debt, and what effects those debts have had on the real wealth -- the annual produce of the land and labor -- of society.
elegant: The first four books explain what has constituted the people's revenue and consumption across different eras and nations. Book V turns to the revenue of the state: first, what expenses government must bear and how costs should be divided between society and particular groups; second, the methods of raising public revenue and the merits of each; third, why nearly all modern governments have taken on debt and what effects that debt has had on national wealth.
summary: Books I through IV explain the sources of national income and consumption. Book V examines government finance: necessary public expenditures, methods of taxation, and the causes and consequences of public debt.
distilled: Book V addresses the three pillars of public finance -- government expenditure, taxation, and public debt -- and their effects on national wealth.
notes: Roadmap entry for Book V, completing the plan of the work. The three-part structure of Book V maps onto modern public finance: expenditure theory, tax theory, and debt theory. 'Revenue of the sovereign, or commonwealth' -- Smith uses both monarchical and republican framings. 'Real wealth, the annual produce of the land and labour' -- Smith's consistent definition of wealth as flow (annual output), not stock. The debt discussion foreshadows Ricardian equivalence debates. Smith's concern with government borrowing remains central to fiscal policy debates today.
